text: The surgeon opens the presacral space by applying traction to the rectum, pushing it cranially, and using gentle blunt dissection. The presacral space is opened up to the level of the coccyx bone, with care taken to avoid damaging the venous plexus. The rectum is mobilized posteriorly, and lateral mobilization begins, ensuring preservation of the inferior hypogastric nerve and its branches near the middle rectal artery.

The surgeon completes the mobilization of the rectum on the posterior aspect, opens the pouch of Douglas, and resects the Denon-Wheeler fascia. The rectum is fully mobilized to the level of the anal canal. On the lateral aspect, the pubococcygeus muscle and its fascia are visible, along with the puborectalis muscle. The inferior hypogastric nerve on the right side is preserved, with similar nerve preservation on the opposite side, ensuring a nerve-sparing prolapse repair.

The surgeon dissects open the anterior longitudinal ligament over the sacral promontory, preparing to anchor the mesh with a tacker. A standard mesh is inserted and anchored using 3-0 ester sutures on the presacral fascia. Additional anchorage is performed along both sides of the puborectalis muscle, with deep bites taken at this level for secure fixation.

The surgeon anchors the posterior aspect of the rectum and pararectal fat to the mesh, ensuring it remains firm over the posterior rectum. The rectum is then pulled up and anchored to the sacral promontory using two tackers. The mesh is reperitonealized, with careful attention to maintaining adequate traction without excessive tightness to avoid post-operative complications.
